title: Star Wars: Fall of the Empire
description: The third Stern Star Wars, built on a classic fan layout. Pick your Rebel characters, spell FORCE to light missions, and score in big chunks through multiballs and character modes.

summary.text: Star Wars: FotE runs on a simple engine: spell F-O-R-C-E by hitting the five standup targets spread across the playfield, and all eight Character Shot inserts light up for Rebel Missions. Pick a character (Han, Luke, Leia, Chewie, Lando, R2, C-3PO, or the Droids) and shoot their assigned shot to run the mission. Meanwhile, hitting the Center Ramp repeatedly advances the Millennium Falcon toward Lightspeed Escape Multiball, and locking three balls at the Luke VUK starts Battle of Hoth Multiball. Character modes and multiballs are your scoring. The game rewards players who pick a lane and stay in it — FORCE → missions → multiball → repeat.

one_shot.name: The Center Ramp
one_shot.description: The center ramp is Han Solo's mission shot, the Falcon's advance shot, and the safest repeatable shot on the table. Feed it from either flipper — it's wide and forgiving. Every time the blue hyperdrive light under the Falcon model is lit, a center ramp shot advances the Falcon toward Lightspeed Escape Multiball. During Han missions it's your primary scoring shot. When you don't know what to do, shoot the center ramp. It almost always does something useful.

key_areas.name: The FORCE Standup Targets
key_areas.location_hint: Five standup targets distributed across the playfield — left side, center, and right side.
key_areas.why_it_matters: Spelling F-O-R-C-E is what turns on the whole game. Until FORCE is complete, the character shot inserts stay dark and you can't start missions. You'll hit these naturally during normal play, but knowing where the unlit letters are lets you clean up the remaining ones intentionally. Every single ball starts here.

key_areas.name: The Center Ramp
key_areas.location_hint: Center of the playfield, wide ramp fed from either flipper.
key_areas.why_it_matters: Han's mission shot, the Falcon's advance shot, and your default when nothing else is lit. More of your scoring runs through this ramp than any other single shot on the table. If you're comfortable here, you're comfortable on this game.

key_areas.name: The Sarlacc Pit
key_areas.location_hint: Right-center, whirlpool ramp behind a drop target. Chewie's shot.
key_areas.why_it_matters: The Sarlacc is the key to surviving multiballs. Ten spins inside the pit lights the action button for add-a-ball — keeping an extra ball in play during Lightspeed or Hoth can double your multiball scoring window. It's also how Jabba's Palace events get triggered, which are some of the biggest point chunks on the table.

key_areas.name: The Luke VUK / AT-AT Shot
key_areas.location_hint: Left side, dead-end shot that feeds back to the left flipper via a spiral around the AT-AT model.
key_areas.why_it_matters: This is the Battle of Hoth multiball lock. Shoot it three times (after lighting locks via the Droids target below the AT-AT) and you're in a full multiball. It's also Luke's mission shot. The VUK return makes it safer than it looks — missed approaches don't drain as often as you'd expect.

hard_box.title: What Makes This Game Hard
hard_box.text: FORCE targets are the most common trap. New players chase them too deliberately and end up shooting at small standups under pressure instead of making comfortable ramp shots. The FORCE letters accumulate naturally during normal play — by the time you've shot the center ramp three or four times and done a few orbit shots, you're usually most of the way there. The other trap is character paralysis: eight characters light up and players stall trying to pick the optimal one instead of just shooting the easiest available shot. Han's center ramp is almost always the right answer when you're unsure.

priorities.title: Let FORCE Happen, Then Clean It Up
priorities.description: Don't open Ball 1 by hunting FORCE Standup Targets — shoot the Center Ramp and left orbit instead. FORCE letters accumulate as the ball bounces around. After three or four comfortable shots, check the display to see which letters are still dark. Then deliberately pick off the missing ones — each FORCE target is a small standup, earnable from the nearby flipper with a controlled shot. When F-O-R-C-E is complete, all eight character inserts light up.

priorities.title: Start a Mission — Pick Han First
priorities.description: When FORCE is complete, the character inserts glow by difficulty: yellow = easy, green = medium, blue = hard. Han Solo (the Center Ramp) is yellow — start there. Shoot the center ramp when Han's insert is lit to begin his mission. During the mission, the display shows which shots score — blue shots progress it, white shots build value. Chewie (Sarlacc Pit) is your backup if Han isn't available: knock down the drop target in front of the pit, then shoot the ramp.

priorities.title: Advance the Falcon Toward Lightspeed
priorities.description: When the blue hyperdrive light under the Falcon model is lit, the Center Ramp advances the Falcon's progress bar toward Lightspeed Escape Multiball. Track your progress on the display — there's a Falcon graphic with a segmented bar that fills from left to right as you hit the ramp. You'll also see the bar build physically on the Falcon toy as lights advance along its hull. This happens naturally if you're already shooting the center ramp for Han missions — you're doing double duty without extra effort.

priorities.title: Start Lightspeed or Battle of Hoth Multiball
priorities.description: You have two paths. Lightspeed: if you advanced the Falcon on Ball 1, keep shooting the Center Ramp — when the Falcon bar fills, Lightspeed Escape Multiball starts automatically. Battle of Hoth: hit the Droids Target (the standup below the AT-AT, left side) to light a lock, then shoot the Luke VUK (left side dead-end shot, feeds back to your flipper) to lock a ball. Three locks starts Hoth. Either multiball is a major scoring opportunity — pick the one that's closer to ready.

priorities.title: In Multiball: Sarlacc Pit for Add-a-Ball First
priorities.description: The moment any multiball starts, your first shot is the Sarlacc Pit (right-center, behind the drop target — knock the target down first, then shoot the ramp). The ball spirals inside and accumulates spins. At 10 spins, the action button lights for an add-a-ball. Getting that extra ball in play early dramatically extends your multiball window and total scoring. Don't chase jackpots first — secure the add-a-ball, then hunt jackpots.

priorities.title: Keep Spelling FORCE Between Multiballs
priorities.description: FORCE resets at the start of each ball. Between multiball attempts, keep shooting comfortable shots and let the FORCE Standup Targets accumulate. The sooner FORCE is complete, the sooner you can layer a character mission on top of whatever multiball you're building toward.

priorities.title: Start the Best Available Multiball Immediately
priorities.description: Check where you are on Lightspeed and Hoth at the Ball 3 plunge. If either is close, go straight for it before doing anything else. Lightspeed: center ramp shots until the bar fills. Hoth: one or two more Luke VUK locks. Multiballs are your biggest scoring windows — getting into one early on Ball 3 gives you the most time to work it.

priorities.title: Run Whatever Missions Are Available
priorities.description: Spell FORCE quickly (hunt the dark letters), then shoot whatever character mission is lit in yellow or green. Han's Center Ramp is always the priority. If you've already run Han's missions, Lando (Death Star Ramp) and Leia (right orbit) are the next easiest. Don't spend Ball 3 reading the display — just shoot the lit characters in easiest-to-hardest order.

priorities.title: If Jabba Is Available, Take It
priorities.description: Jabba's Palace events are triggered by hitting the Sarlacc Pit drop target twice — the ball then spirals into the pit and you select an event with the action button. On Ball 3 with nothing to lose, Rancor is the right pick: it's a combo mode with no timer where you hit orange shots in sequence. No clock pressure, solid point chunks, and the Sarlacc pit is a comfortable shot anyway.

items.title: Center Ramp Is Your Safe Shot
items.description: When the ball is moving fast and you're not sure what to hit, shoot the Center Ramp. It advances at least one thing (Han mission, Falcon bar, or FORCE letters in the upper lanes) on almost every ball state. It's wide enough to hit under pressure and almost never puts you in a dangerous position.

items.title: The Sarlacc Drop Target Is a Drain Trap
items.description: The drop target in front of the Sarlacc Pit sits close to the right flipper. A weak shot at it can bounce back fast and catch you mid-flipper. Hit it with conviction or don't hit it at all. When the target is down and the pit is open, the ramp shot is safe — the ball returns to your left flipper via the spiral.

items.title: Trap and Read the Insert Colors
items.description: After FORCE completes, eight character inserts light up simultaneously and it's easy to panic-fire at whatever looks brightest. Trap the ball on a flipper, take two seconds to find the yellow insert (easiest mission), and shoot that one. Reading inserts is faster than it sounds once you practice it.

items.title: Orbits Feed Back Safely
items.description: The Left Orbit and Right Orbit both feed back to the opposite flipper and advance FORCE letters in the upper lanes. When you're uncertain between shots, an orbit is a low-risk choice that makes progress. They also light the skill shot multiplier lanes, which is free value.

items.title: Don't Hunt FORCE Targets Aggressively
items.description: The FORCE Standup Targets are small and spread around the playfield. Hunting them one-by-one at the start of a ball often leads to awkward angles, missed shots, and unexpected drains. Shoot your comfortable shots (center ramp, orbits) and let FORCE fill passively — then pick off any stragglers with deliberate shots.

items.title: Don't Stack Multiballs Until the Loop Feels Automatic
items.description: Lightspeed and Hoth can technically be stacked — start Lightspeed before locking the third Hoth ball. It's a massive scoring window. It's also a lot to manage. For league play, run one multiball at a time until you can reliably hit jackpot shots under pressure. Stacking is in Level Up territory.

items.title: Don't Chase Hard Characters on Ball 1
items.description: Blue-coded characters (hard difficulty) have awkward shot locations and tight timing windows. Luke, R2, and C-3PO all have missions that require shots most league players can't hit consistently under pressure. Stay in yellow and green until the game feels comfortable. Han and Chewie will carry you through most of a league game.

items.title: Don't Force a Death Star Multiball
items.description: Death Star Multiball requires lowering a target at the top of the Death Star Ramp, then locking three balls there. The ramp is on the left side and the lock target is finicky. Unless you're on Ball 3 and both Lightspeed and Hoth are unavailable, don't make Death Star your plan.

between_us.label: Pick a Lane and Stay In It
between_us.text: The biggest mistake on this game is trying to do everything. Eight character missions, three multiballs, Jabba events — it's a lot. Players who score well pick one multiball to build toward (usually Lightspeed via the center ramp) and run missions that use that same shot. Han + Lightspeed via the center ramp is the most coherent single-lane strategy on the table.

between_us.label: The Action Button Actually Matters Here
between_us.text: Stern games with action buttons are hit-or-miss on whether the button does anything useful. On SW:FotE it matters. During Jabba's Palace, it selects your event as the ball spirals in the Sarlacc. During multiball, it collects add-a-ball once the Sarlacc pit hits 10 spins. Watch the button indicator in the lower-left of the display — when it's lit, press it.

between_us.label: Pro vs Premium: The Differences You'll Notice
between_us.text: Most league machines are Pros. The Premium adds a moving Death Star target and physical Hoth snowfield toy. Strategically the games play the same — the Pro Death Star Ramp just has a fixed target at the top. The core loop (FORCE → missions → multiballs) is identical on both models.

between_us.label: JEDI Qualification Is Subtle
between_us.text: Completing both orbit shots (left and right) advances JEDI letters in the upper lanes. Spelling JEDI qualifies a shot multiplier that applies to your next major shot. Most players don't notice this happening — but if you see JEDI complete on the display while you're setting up a jackpot collect, that jackpot is about to score much bigger. The orbits are building this passively the whole game.

playfield_callouts.name: FORCE Standup Targets
playfield_callouts.description: Five standup targets distributed across the playfield — two on the left, one in the center, two on the right. Each lights one letter of F-O-R-C-E on the display. They're individually earnable from the nearby flipper but are small targets. Completing all five lights all eight character mission inserts.

playfield_callouts.name: Center Ramp
playfield_callouts.description: The wide ramp in the center of the playfield. Fed from either flipper — the right flipper gives a cleaner approach. Han Solo's primary mission shot, the Falcon advance shot, and the safest repeatable shot on the table. The Millennium Falcon model sits above the ramp exit.

playfield_callouts.name: Sarlacc Pit
playfield_callouts.description: A whirlpool ramp on the right-center of the playfield. A drop target (Salacious Crumb) blocks the entrance — knock it down with a direct hit first, then shoot the ramp. The ball spirals inside and accumulates spins. Ten spins lights the action button for add-a-ball. Chewie's mission shot. Feeds back to the left flipper via the spiral exit.

playfield_callouts.name: Luke VUK / AT-AT Shot
playfield_callouts.description: A dead-end VUK on the left side of the playfield. The ball spirals around the AT-AT model and returns to the left flipper. Luke's mission shot and the Battle of Hoth ball lock. Easier than it looks from the left flipper — the spiral return means errant shots don't drain as often as expected.

playfield_callouts.name: Droids Target
playfield_callouts.description: A standup target on the left side of the playfield, below and in front of the AT-AT model. Hit it to light a Hoth lock at the Luke VUK. The Droids mission also uses this target. Earnable from the left flipper.

playfield_callouts.name: Death Star Ramp
playfield_callouts.description: Left-side ramp with a target at the top. Lando's mission shot. Hit the target at the top to lower it, revealing a ball lock behind it. Lock three balls for Death Star Multiball. On Premium models, the target mechanism is motorized; on Pro, it's a fixed target.

playfield_callouts.name: Left Orbit
playfield_callouts.description: The left orbit shot, fed from the right flipper as a backhand or the left flipper with a firm hold. Sends the ball around the top of the playfield and feeds back to the right flipper. Advances FORCE letters in the upper lanes and builds JEDI progress. Leia's mission shot.

playfield_callouts.name: Right Orbit
playfield_callouts.description: The right orbit shot, fed from the left flipper. Sends the ball around the top-right and feeds back to the left flipper. Advances FORCE letters and JEDI progress. R2-D2's mission shot. Slightly tighter than the left orbit but still earnable.

sources.title: Kineticist — 'May the Force Be With You' Tutorial
sources.description: Primary source. Noah Crable's comprehensive written tutorial covering character perks, mode details, multiball mechanics, Jabba events, and tournament strategies.

sources.title: The Pinball Company — Travis's SW:FotE Walkthrough
sources.description: Travis's video walkthrough of the full game loop, multiball strategies, and Sarlacc pit mechanics. Primary source for the Sarlacc add-a-ball priority and center ramp philosophy.

sources.title: Tilt Forums — Star Wars: FotE Rulesheet
sources.description: Community wiki rulesheet with detailed scoring data and code version tracking.

sources.title: Stern Pinball — Official Rulesheet v5
sources.description: Manufacturer rulesheet covering FORCE target difficulty levels, skill shot mechanics, and multiball rules.

sources.title: Pinside — Star Wars: FotE Strategy Thread
sources.description: Community strategy discussion including tournament strategies and JEDI multiplier optimization tips.

chat_starters: Which character should I pick first when FORCE is complete?
How do I start Battle of Hoth multiball?
What does the Sarlacc Pit actually do for me?

level_up.intro: Once the FORCE → mission → multiball loop feels automatic and you're consistently getting into Lightspeed or Hoth, these strategies will push your scores significantly higher.

tips.title: Stack Lightspeed + Hoth
tips.description: Start Lightspeed Escape before collecting your 3rd Hoth lock — build Lightspeed via the center ramp, get two Hoth locks banked, then start Lightspeed. Once Lightspeed is running, shoot the Luke VUK for the 3rd lock. Both multiballs run simultaneously. Build Lightspeed jackpots first, then let Hoth add-a-balls extend the window. This is the single biggest scoring play on the table.

tips.title: Shot Multiplier Timing via JEDI
tips.description: Spelling JEDI through the upper orbit lanes applies a multiplier to your next major shot. The orbits build JEDI passively during normal play — watch the display for the JEDI complete indicator. If you see it complete right before a jackpot collect or mode completion shot, that shot is about to score dramatically more. Trap the ball, confirm the multiplier is ready, then shoot. This is the biggest single-shot scoring lever for advancing players.

tips.title: Jabba's Palace: Rancor Over Everything
tips.description: When Jabba's Palace activates (two Sarlacc drop target hits), use the action button to select Rancor as the ball spirals in the pit. Rancor is a combo mode with no timer — hit orange shots in sequence for escalating values. Unlike the other Jabba events, there's no clock pressure, and the orange shots are all readable in advance. Rancor consistently outscores the timed Jabba events for average players.

tips.title: Han's Palace Mode for Unlimited Scoring
tips.description: Han's Jabba's Palace mode (separate from the Sarlacc event) has unlimited scoring potential as long as you keep hitting the drop target to reset its timer. The scoring ceiling is theoretically unlimited — strong players have banked enormous scores here. To access it, complete Han's full mission set first, then qualify the Palace via the Sarlacc. This is deep into advanced territory but worth knowing exists.
